attributes.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents to the antenatal clinic at 36 weeks gestation with a one-day history of reduced fetal movements. This is her first pregnancy. She has no significant medical history and takes only pregnancy multivitamins. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. The fundal height is appropriate for gestational age. A cardiotocograph (CTG) is performed.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best defines an acceleration on a cardiotocograph?
options: A decrease in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute for 15 seconds
An increase in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute or more above baseline, lasting 15 seconds or more
An increase in fetal heart rate of 5 beats per minute above baseline, lasting 15 seconds
An increase in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute above baseline, lasting 5 seconds
An increase in fetal heart rate of 5 beats per minute above baseline, lasting 5 seconds
attributes.explanation:
In the context of cardiotocograph (CTG) interpretation, an increase in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute or more above baseline, lasting 15 seconds or more is the correct definition of an acceleration. This is a key feature of CTG interpretation and is considered a reassuring sign of fetal wellbeing.

Accelerations indicate that the fetus is responsive and has an intact central nervous system and autonomic control. They are typically seen in association with fetal movement and are a sign that the fetus is well-oxygenated and not under stress.

A decrease in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute for 15 seconds would be considered a deceleration, not an acceleration. Decelerations can be normal or indicate fetal distress, depending on their timing and characteristics.

An increase in fetal heart rate of 5 beats per minute above baseline, lasting 15 seconds does not meet the criteria for an acceleration. While the duration is sufficient (15 seconds), the magnitude of the increase (5 beats per minute) is not enough to be classified as an acceleration, which requires an increase of at least 15 beats per minute.

An increase in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute above baseline, lasting 5 seconds does not meet the criteria for an acceleration. While the magnitude of the increase is sufficient (15 beats per minute), the duration (5 seconds) is too short to be classified as an acceleration, which requires a duration of at least 15 seconds.

An increase in fetal heart rate of 5 beats per minute above baseline, lasting 5 seconds does not meet the criteria for an acceleration in either magnitude or duration.

In the clinical scenario presented, where a woman reports reduced fetal movements, the presence of accelerations on the CTG would be particularly reassuring. However, the overall CTG pattern, including baseline rate, variability, and presence or absence of decelerations, should be evaluated together with the clinical picture to assess fetal wellbeing.
learningPoints: Accelerations on a cardiotocograph (CTG) are reassuring signs of fetal wellbeing.
An acceleration is defined as a transient increase in fetal heart rate of 15 beats per minute or more above the baseline, lasting for 15 seconds or more.
Reduced fetal movements warrant careful assessment, including CTG monitoring.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists. Intrapartum fetal surveillance: clinical guideline. 5th ed. Melbourne: RANZCOG; 2021. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Intrapartum-Fetal-Surveillance.pdf
